description: When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - When configuring the same BGP neighbor under both IPv4 and IPv6 address families on a router, if a maximum prefix limit is set on one address family (like IPv4), exceeding that limit can potentially impact the BGP session for the other address family (IPv6) as well.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) status indicated a duplicate for IPv6 anycast gateway configuration. As a workaround, configure an active gateway IPv6 address and SVI IPv6 address, as this can prevent DAD issues. Issuing the commands interface shut followed by interface no-shut can help in resolving the issue.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for ARP?

Feature Caveat: ARP - Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) status indicated a duplicate for IPv6 anycast gateway configuration. As a workaround, configure an active gateway IPv6 address and SVI IPv6 address, as this can prevent DAD issues. Issuing the commands interface shut followed by interface no-shut can help in resolving the issue.

description: After an issue with duplicate IPs on two VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs) is resolved, the kernel will have the ARP entry, but the output of the show arp and show evpn mac-ip commands do not show that IP. As a workaround, flap the physical interface.

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what limitation is mentioned for ARP?

Feature Caveat: ARP - After an issue with duplicate IPs on two VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs) is resolved, the kernel will have the ARP entry, but the output of the show arp and show evpn mac-ip commands do not show that IP. As a workaround, flap the physical interface.

description: The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp

messages.content: For 8325 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what limitation is mentioned for BGP?

Feature Caveat: BGP - The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve nexthop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example: Copy router bgp.

description: Symptom: DHCP-Server configuration status becomes stuck in pending state after configuration changes are pushed from Aruba Central, preventing proper DHCP service operation. Scenario: This issue occurs when administrators make configuration changes using Aruba Central, either through Central template groups or the UI group multi-edit option. The DHCP Server configuration status may become stuck in a pending state. This issue can occur even when the configuration changes are completely unrelated to DHCP services. The pending status persists indefinitely, requiring manual intervention.

description: Symptom: The sflow source ip configuration gets corrupted in the cache and the following debug log can be seen: 2025-11-04:11:28:04.603048 | sflowd | LOG_ERR | CDTR | 1 | SFLOW | SFLOW_CONFIG | Failed to set source IPv4. Scenario: This issue can occur when source ip is configured for sFlow and collectors are added and sFlow is enabled globally. Workaround: Unconfigure and re-configure the sFlow source ip configuration.
symptom: The sflow source ip configuration gets corrupted in the cache and the following debug log can be seen: 2025-11-04:11:28:04.603048 | sflowd | LOG_ERR | CDTR | 1 | SFLOW | SFLOW_CONFIG | Failed to set source IPv4

description: Symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one. Scenario: This issue may occur when deleting an existing SNMPv3 user and re-adding the same username with a different 'auth-pass'; authentication succeeds only with the original password. Workaround: Create a new SNMPv3 user with the desired password and do not reuse the original username.
symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one.
scenario: This issue may occur when deleting an existing SNMPv3 user and re-adding the same username with a different 'auth-pass'; authentication succeeds only with the original password.
workaround: Create a new SNMPv3 user with the desired password and do not reuse the original username.

description: Symptom: CPU-transmitted packets are not subjected to actions configured in policies applied to route-only ports on egress. This includes traffic originating directly from the switch, such as pings, as well as traffic forwarded to the CPU as part of the initial learning of a traffic flow. Scenario: This issue occurs when a policy is applied to a route-only port on egress and CPU-transmitted packets egress the port. Workaround: If the policy has any drop actions, use an egress ACL on the port to block the traffic instead.
symptom: CPU-transmitted packets are not subjected to actions configured in policies applied to route-only ports on egress. This includes traffic originating directly from the switch, such as pings, as well as traffic forwarded to the CPU as part of the initial learning of a traffic flow.

description: Symptom: DHCP-Server configuration status becomes stuck in pending state after configuration changes are pushed from Aruba Central, preventing proper DHCP service operation. Scenario: This issue occurs when administrators make configuration changes using Aruba Central, either through Central template groups or the UI group multi-edit option. The DHCP Server configuration status may become stuck in a pending state. This issue can occur even when the configuration changes are completely unrelated to DHCP services. The pending status persists indefinitely, requiring manual intervention.

description: Symptom: The sflow source ip configuration gets corrupted in the cache and the following debug log can be seen: 2025-11-04:11:28:04.603048 | sflowd | LOG_ERR | CDTR | 1 | SFLOW | SFLOW_CONFIG | Failed to set source IPv4. Scenario: This issue can occur when source ip is configured for sFlow and collectors are added and sFlow is enabled globally. Workaround: Unconfigure and re-configure the sFlow source ip configuration.

description: Symptom: SNMPv3 user password changes do not take effect, after deleting and re-creating the user with a new password, the device still accepts the old password and rejects the new one. Scenario: This issue may occur when deleting an existing SNMPv3 user and re-adding the same username with a different 'auth-pass'; authentication succeeds only with the original password. Workaround: Create a new SNMPv3 user with the desired password and do not reuse the original username.

description: Symptom: CPU-transmitted packets are not subjected to actions configured in policies applied to route-only ports on egress. This includes traffic originating directly from the switch, such as pings, as well as traffic forwarded to the CPU as part of the initial learning of a traffic flow. Scenario: This issue occurs when a policy is applied to a route-only port on egress and CPU-transmitted packets egress the port. Workaround: If the policy has any drop actions, use an egress ACL on the port to block the traffic instead.

description: Symptom: CPU-transmitted packets are not subjected to actions configured in policies applied to route-only ports on egress. This includes traffic originating directly from the switch, such as pings, as well as traffic forwarded to the CPU as part of the initial learning of a traffic flow. Scenario: This issue occurs when a policy is applied to a route-only port on egress and CPU-transmitted packets egress the port. Workaround: If the policy has any drop actions, use an egress ACL on the port to block the traffic instead.

Classifier (Bug ID 408826): The documented workaround is: If the policy has any drop actions, use an egress ACL on the port to block the traffic instead.
